Digital Services

The AI4SoilHealth Toolbox includes not only field and laboratory methods, but also digital services that help users record, organise, visualise, and interpret soil health information.

These services support the practical use of the toolbox by connecting local measurements with broader digital workflows.

Why digital services matter

Digital services make it easier to:

  • organise soil health information,
  • link observations to location,
  • compare records,
  • visualise data,
  • produce user-friendly outputs,
  • and combine local evidence with broader spatial context.

Main digital components

AI4SoilHealth App

The app is the main user-facing digital environment of the toolbox. Public AI4SoilHealth materials describe it as a way to connect field observations, laboratory information, and wider spatial context. The SHDC documentation also presents the app architecture as a system linking Tier 1 Data Cube information, Tier 2 field measurements, and Tier 3 laboratory data. (AI4SoilHealth home; SHDC specifications)

Geolocated field data collection

This function helps users create records linked to specific places. It can support observations, measurements, sensor information, and images.

Visualisation interface

Digital visualisation helps users explore information through maps, charts, tables, and filtered views.

Reporting and soil health cards

The digital environment can also help turn data into more readable outputs that are easier to communicate and share.

Supporting spatial and contextual service

The Soil Health Data Cube provides a broader digital context that helps users interpret local observations and measurements.

Wider digital ecosystem

The official AI4SoilHealth and SHDC sites also point users to a wider ecosystem of digital resources, including:

  • the main project website,
  • the Soil Health Data Cube interface and technical documentation,
  • project repositories on GitHub,
  • videos and webinars on YouTube,
  • and public communications through LinkedIn, X/Twitter, and Bluesky. (AI4SoilHealth home; SHDC welcome page)

What users can gain from the digital environment

By using the digital services, users may be able to:

  • keep records more systematically,
  • combine measurements and observations,
  • visualise results more clearly,
  • connect local issues with a wider context,
  • and generate outputs that are easier to use in communication and decision processes.

Think of the digital services as the part of the toolbox that helps users turn measurements and observations into usable information.

Twitter LinkedIn YouTube Bluesky
